transport_strategy: Overnight sleeper trains between Jaipur–Jodhpur and Jodhpur–Jaisalmer save time and accommodation; use private cabs for short hops like Jaisalmer–Udaipur. Within cities, hire auto-rickshaws or rent scooters for flexibility.
seasonal_tips: Peak season — book trains, hotels, and marquee experiences well ahead.
Nights get cold this time of year — pack layers/thermals, especially in desert or hill areas.

stay_by_city.Jaipur: Heritage haveli in the old city near Johari Bazaar
stay_by_city.Jodhpur: Heritage haveli in the blue city, close to Clock Tower
stay_by_city.Jaisalmer: Heritage desert haveli near the fort
stay_by_city.Udaipur: Heritage haveli on the banks of Lake Pichola

discoveries.title: Mehrangarh Fort
discoveries.body: Impressive fort in Jodhpur, the 'Blue City', with seven gates and a museum of Marwar royal palanquins and Rajput weaponry, founded by Rao Jodha in 1459 Best for: history enthusiasts, architecture lovers.

discoveries.title: palace of the royal family of Udaipur

days.title: Pink City Arrival & Old Town Immersion
days.description: Touch down in Jaipur and head straight into the walled old city — narrow lanes, hand-painted havelis, and the scent of spices from roadside stalls. Start with Hawa Mahal at golden hour, then wander into the bazaars, and wind down with a thali at a dhaba-style eatery that doesn't cater only to tourists.
highlights: Hawa Mahal at sunset — golden light on pink sandstone
daal baati at a local dhaba
Walk: Jaipur Old City's painted facades

stops.name: Hawa Mahal Jaipur
stops.description: Climb the back stairs for the best view of the honeycomb facade — the morning rush hasn't hit yet, but the evening light here is perfect. Locals come late too, so you'll blend in.

stops.name: Jaipur Old City
stops.description: Wander the tight lanes of Johari Bazaar and Tripolia — watch for lacquer bangles, camel leather, and block-print textiles. This is where real life happens, not just photo ops. Skip the branded 'heritage walks' and let yourself get lost.

stops.name: daal baati
stops.description: Eat daal baati churma at Shree Lal Cutlery near Sindhi Camp — no frills, all flavor. It's a dhaba favorite, not a tourist buffet. Order extra ghee.

days.title: Fort, Instruments, and Royal Palaces
days.description: Start early at Amer Fort — climb up on foot or take a jeep, but beat the tour buses. Then head to Jantar Mantar, right after, before circling back to City Palace's courtyards. Wrap the day in a quieter corner of the old city with craft shopping and chai.
highlights: Amer Fort's Sheesh Mahal — go before 10 AM
Jantar Mantar’s sundial accuracy
Buy: block-print scarves at local shop near City Palace

stops.name: Amer Fort
stops.description: Pink and yellow sandstone fortress founded in 1592 — the Sheesh Mahal's mirror work is best seen in morning light. Skip the elephant ride; the walk up is more authentic.

stops.name: Jantar Mantar
stops.description: 18th-century astronomical observatory with the world’s largest sundial. The precision still works — locals check birthdays here. Guide tip: ask for the monsoon alignment demo.

stops.name: City Palace
stops.description: Rajput-Mughal fusion palace with courtyards and mubarak gates. Don’t miss the textile museum — some of the finest royal turbans and skirts ever made.

days.title: Museums, Crafts, and Local Flavors
days.description: Begin at Albert Hall, Rajasthan’s oldest museum, then dive into niche bazaars most tours skip. End the day with a quick visit to a lesser-known food spot before boarding the overnight train to Jodhpur.
highlights: Albert Hall’s Egyptian mummy — yes, really
Shop: blue pottery at Kripal Kumbh
Train: Sleeper berth to Jodhpur — pack a shawl

stops.name: Albert Hall Museum
stops.description: Copper dome Indo-Saracenic museum with mummies, taxidermy leopards, and vintage arms. The garden outside is a cool retreat — have chai at the kiosk.

stops.name: Kripal Kumbh
stops.description: Family-run blue pottery workshop near Adarsh Nagar — watch artisans throw pots and buy directly. Avoid the bazaar stalls with mass-produced sets.

stops.name: Choki Dhani Jaipur
stops.description: You’ve seen the signs — it’s touristy but does serve authentic daal baati live-cooked over fire. Only go if you missed it yesterday. Skip if you want pure local vibe.

days.title: Blue City Arrival & Fort First Light
days.description: Arrive in Jodhpur by morning, settle into your haveli, then head straight to Mehrangarh — arrive at opening to have the ramparts nearly to yourself. After lunch, drift through the blue alleys near the clock tower, and end with a rooftop view as the city turns indigo.
highlights: Mehrangarh Fort at opening — cool stone, no crowds
Clock Tower market buzz
Rooftop chai with blue-hued lanes below

stops.name: Mehrangarh Fort
stops.description: Perched on a 125m hill, founded by Rao Jodha in 1459. The museum has royal palanquins and swords — but save time for the ramparts’ view over the blue houses. Don’t touch the iron spikes on the gate — it’s a local superstition.

stops.name: Clock Tower Market
stops.description: The heart of Jodhpur’s blue city — leather juttis, spices, and pyjamas sold in family-run shops. Bargain gently — these are livelihoods, not souvenirs.

stops.name: Rooftop at Raas Devli
stops.description: Chai with a view — ask your haveli staff to point you to a quiet rooftop nearby. The blue paint comes from turpentine and indigo, meant to repel insects — now it’s the city’s soul.

days.title: Temples, Local Eats & Rural Roads
days.description: Start with a short drive to Mandore Gardens, former capital’s ruins and quiet cenotaphs. Then hit a dhaba for thali before an afternoon exploring quieter lanes west of the fort. End with sunset from a less-visited ridge.
highlights: Mandore’s ghostly royal cenotaphs
Thali at Shri Nath Dhaba — no AC, all flavor
Drive: countryside roads with camel carts and farmers

stops.name: Mandore Gardens
stops.description: Ancient capital of Marwar with garden cenotaphs and temple ruins. The quiet here is a relief after the fort’s crowds. Look for the carved stone monkeys at the entrance.

stops.name: Shri Nath Dhaba
stops.description: No frills, no menu — just thali with daal, baati, kachori, and buttermilk. Pay ₹120 each. Locals fill the front tables — sit there.

stops.name: Sardar Market
stops.description: Less touristy than Clock Tower — here, shop for dried mint, black salt, and woolen caps. A fruit stall sells ber (desert plum) — try one, it’s tart and seasonal.

days.title: Cuisine, Crafts & Water Risks Reminder
days.description: Morning visit to a local sweet shop for mawa kachori, then a craft workshop. Reaffirm clean water habits before catching the overnight train to Jaisalmer — this leg is long, so board early.
highlights: Mawa kachori at Jodhpur Sweets
Craft: Hand-block printing demo
Warning: Stick to bottled water — Jodhpur tapwater risk

stops.name: Jodhpur Sweets
stops.description: Try mawa kachori — stuffed with reduced milk and sugar, fried crisp. It’s heavy, so split one. Pair with masala chai. They pack it well for travel.

stops.name: Block Printing Workshop
stops.description: Learn hand-block printing at Dedhia Haveli workshop — they use natural dyes. Buy a scarf; it supports artisans directly.

stops.name: Overnight train to Jaisalmer
stops.description: Express sleeper train (Jodhpur–Jaisalmer). Board by 7 PM — bring snacks, water, and a shawl. Nights are cold in the desert, even in peak season.

days.title: Golden Fort & Desert Welcome
days.description: Arrive in Jaisalmer by morning, check into your desert haveli, then step into the fort complex before the heat hits. Explore the Jain temples and merchant havelis, then cool off in the afternoon with a rooftop tea and views over the sandstone skyline.
highlights: Walk the Jaisalmer Fort battlements at 9 AM
Jain temples’ marble filigree
Rooftop chill at 12 noon — wind off the dunes

stops.name: Jaisalmer Fort
stops.description: Living fort — 2,000 people still live inside. The yellow sandstone glows at dawn and dusk. Skip the camel safari pushers at the gate; they’re relentless.

stops.name: Patwon Ki Haveli
stops.description: Five-grand-haveli complex with the finest stone jharokhas. Go around back for a quiet photo angle — most tourists stop at the front steps.

stops.name: Saffron Rooftop Café
stops.description: Chai and paratha with AC and breeze. They use local ghee and serve ber sherbet in season. Staff know good desert photography spots — ask.

days.title: Dunes, Desert Culture & Local Thali
days.description: After a late start, visit a desert village via local road, then gear up for a sunset camel ride into Sam Sand Dunes. Return for a dhaba-style thali — not the tourist buffet — before night settles on the desert.
highlights: Drive through Bada Bagh’s royal cenotaphs
Camel ride to Sam Dunes at golden hour
Thali at Desert Boys Dhaba — no fake folk dance

stops.name: Bada Bagh
stops.description: Six royal chhatris (cenotaphs) on a plain, framed by dunes. Best in late afternoon light. Few tourists stay this long.

stops.name: Sam Sand Dunes
stops.description: One-hour camel ride into the dunes — insist on no music, no forced dance. The real moment is silence at sunset, sand turning gold, and stars popping out.

stops.name: Desert Boys Dhaba
stops.description: Eat thali here — no neon lights, no stage. They serve ker sangri and dal baati. Staff are from nearby villages — ask about desert life.

days.title: Local Life & Road to Udaipur
days.description: Spend the morning in Gadisar Lake, a quiet reservoir with temples and migratory birds. Then hit the road early — it’s a long cab ride to Udaipur, but the countryside unfolds in desert scrub, villages, and sudden green belts.
highlights: Gadisar Lake’s turtle feeding at 8 AM
Drive through rural Rajasthan — real heartbeat
Sunset en route near Chittorgarh Fort far off

stops.name: Gadisar Lake
stops.description: Man-made lake from 1360, now a peaceful spot with temples and birdwatchers. Feed the turtles — kids sell corn at the gate.

stops.name: Road trip to Udaipur
stops.description: 6-hour cab ride via NH12 — pack snacks and water. The landscape shifts from golden scrub to river valleys. Stop at a dhaba in Bhilwara for lunch later.

stops.name: Lunch in Jaisalmer
stops.description: Anchor slot suggested by the planner — swap for a specific spot in Jaisalmer you've already saved.

days.title: Lake Palaces & Royal Reflections
days.description: Arrive in Udaipur by late afternoon, check into your haveli by the lake, then take a boat to Jag Mandir as dusk falls. The water reflects the city lights and palaces — it’s not staged, it’s just how it looks.
highlights: Boat to Jag Mandir at sunset — rippling lights
Walk along Lake Pichola’s promenade
Dinner with view of City Palace lights

stops.name: Lunch in Udaipur
stops.description: Anchor slot suggested by the planner — swap for a specific spot in Udaipur you've already saved.

stops.name: Lake Pichola
stops.description: Man-made lake with island palaces. Take the last public boat (₹500) to Jag Mandir — skip private cruises. The backside of the City Palace at dusk is the best photo.

stops.name: Ambrai Restaurant
stops.description: Rooftop multi-cuisine, but order the dal baati and laal maas. It's touristy but has the best view — worth one splurge. Book terrace table.

days.title: Palace, Museum & Farewell
days.description: Finish with Udaipur’s City Palace — not just royal rooms, but courtyards that open to the lake. Then visit the palace museum before heading to the station or airport. End with a final chai at a bench facing the water.
highlights: City Palace courtyards — morning light on arches
Museum of Royal Gowns and Armour
Chai at Gangaur Ghat — locals wave on boat rides

stops.name: Old Udaipur market walk
stops.description: Anchor slot suggested by the planner — swap for a specific spot in Udaipur you've already saved.

stops.name: Gangaur Ghat
stops.description: Sip chai at the steps where brides once walked to the lake. Boats glide by — some with saris flapping, others selling marigolds.

stops.name: Lunch in Udaipur
stops.description: Anchor slot suggested by the planner — swap for a specific spot in Udaipur you've already saved.
